literal: wrongly, falsely; an indeclinable used adjectivally
invariant_definition: There is no shared invariant; the term marks a fault-line. In Advaita technical usage, *mithyā* names a third ontological status: neither *sat* like *brahman* nor *asat* like the hare's horn, but *anirvacanīya*, dependent on an *adhiṣṭhāna*, and subject to sublation by a stronger cognition. The stock examples are shell-silver, rope-snake, and dream objects, and the world is said to be *mithyā* in that technical sense, not in the sense of sheer nullity. That point matters because critics regularly hear 'world is *mithyā*' as 'world is unreal' or 'non-existent', which is exactly what Advaita denies. Non-Advaita schools either reject the category as incoherent or restrict falsity to error without elevating it into a third mode of being. The indispensable constraint is therefore simple: *mithyā* must not be collapsed into *asat*, and Advaita's deployment of the term must not be misread as nihilism or world-denial.

per_school.definition: *Mithyā* names what appears and enters *vyavahāra* without being either sheer *sat* or sheer *asat*; Maṇḍana accordingly treats *avidyā/māyā* as *mithyāvabhāsa* and *anirvacanīyā*, since absolute non-being could not sustain practical commerce [[BS 1.1](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)]]. Padmapāda makes the point lexical: in *mithyājñāna*, the member *mithyā* itself states *anirvacanīyatā*, and the appearance of a *mithyārtha* requires admission of *avidyāśakti* [[PañcP V.15.2](cite://padmapada/pancapadika/V.15.2)] [[PañcP VI.16.5](cite://padmapada/pancapadika/VI.16.5)]]. Later Advaita systematizes the thesis by treating liberating knowledge as the cessation of beginningless *mithyājñāna* and by making the proof of *dvaita-mithyātva* the opening task of dialectic [[PV p. 164c](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/p. 164c)] [[AS 1a](cite://madhusudana/advaita-siddhi/Prathama-pariccheda, Section 1a)] [[AS 1k](cite://madhusudana/advaita-siddhi/Prathama-pariccheda, Section 1k)]].

For the Śrīvaiṣṇava line, *prapañca-sadbhāva* is not canceled by *advaita-śruti*; "without a second" excludes a peer or superior to Brahman, not the real order of *cit* and *acit* dependent on him [[Svs 31-35](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/31-35)]]. Accordingly, the delusion to be removed is the thesis that Brahman itself becomes the transmigrating sufferer under *upādhi*; Rāmānuja instead construes the Vedānta corpus as teaching the body-transcendent *jīva*, the *antaryāmin* Paramātman, and worship culminating in Brahman-experience [[VS §2](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§2)] [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]].

*Mithyātva* is rejected as an incoherent middle-status: if the probandum is taken as *sadasad-vilakṣaṇa*, the inference proves what is already conceded, and if *anirvacanīya* is unestablished, the qualifier itself fails [[MKh 239.4](cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 4)] [[MKh 239.3](cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 3)]]. Madhva therefore denies that the mere word *mithyā* can secure *anirvacanīyatā* or furnish a shared *sāmānya* from which a valid *anumāna* could proceed [[Tattvodyota, Mithyā](cite://madhva/tattvodyota/Word *Mithyā* Does Not Help)] [[Tattvodyota, No Shared Universal](cite://madhva/tattvodyota/Shared Universal of *Mithyātva*)]]. The realist remainder is explicit: Hari is the cause of a wholly real world, and falsehood belongs to *mithyādarśana* in the knower rather than to the ontic standing of *prapañca* [[Nyāyāmṛta 1](cite://vyasatirtha/nyayamrta/1)] [[VTV 3.10](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.10)]].

In Jīva Gosvāmin's handling, *abheda-śāstra* is not license to collapse plurality into a *mithyā* world-order; non-difference texts are to be coordinated only after difference and non-difference have been reconciled through Bhagavān's *acintya-śakti* [[SatS §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42)] [[SatS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]]. Bondage is fixed instead by the asymmetry between Bhagavān as *māyā-āśraya* and the jīva as *māyā-mohita*, so the pertinent falsehood is delusive apprehension within saṃsāra rather than the unreality of Bhagavān's śaktis or of devotional relation [[SatS §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)]].

For Nimbārka, the bound self is beginninglessly conjoined with *māyā*, yet it remains a real and eternal *aṃśa* of Bhagavān; bondage is therefore not grounded in a third ontological status of the self [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)] [[Gītā 15.7a](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/15.7a)]]. The school's scriptural *aṃśa*-grammar treats plurality as real within dependence, not as a merely *mithyā* appearance awaiting sublation [[VPS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)] [[ChU 3.12.6](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/Upaniṣad 3.12.6)]].
primary_loci: Nimbārka, cited Chāndogya Upaniṣad 3.12.6 and Bhagavad-Gītā 15.7a

per_school.definition: Vallabha's line rejects *mithyātva* as a verdict on the world because Brahman itself is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa*, with name and form belonging to the effect-side of a real manifestation [[AnuBh 1.1.4a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4a)] [[AnuBh 1.1.4d](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4d)]]. *Jagat* is therefore *avikṛta-pariṇāma*: Brahman really becomes the effect-form while remaining unmodified, as gold becomes ornaments without loss of its own substance [[AnuBh 1.4.26a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26a)] [[AnuBh 1.4.26b](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26b)] [[AnuBh 1.4.26c](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26c)]]. Ignorance may cease in the aspirant, but that cessation prepares attainment of Puruṣottama rather than disclosure of a world previously demoted to *mithyā* [[AnuBh 1.1.12-19a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19a)] [[AnuBh 1.1.12-19b](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19b)]].

Sāṃkhya does not deploy *mithyā* as a third ontological status; its operative category is *viparyaya*, since bondage is said to arise from *viparyaya* and release from *jñāna* [[SK 44](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/44)]]. The *pratyayasarga* is accordingly classified under *viparyaya*, *aśakti*, *tuṣṭi*, and *siddhi*, with *viparyaya* itself divided into the five heads of *tamas*, *moha*, *mahāmoha*, *tāmisra*, and *andhatāmisra* [[SK 46-48](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/46-48)]]. The counter-cognition is *aviparyaya*: through *tattvābhyāsa* there arises a *viśuddha*, *kevala* jñāna, i.e. discriminative knowledge of *guṇa* and *puruṣa* [[SK 64](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/64)]].

Yoga makes the term explicit: *viparyaya* is *mithyājñāna*, a *vṛtti* established in what is not that object's form [[YS 1.8](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.8)]]. Vyāsa explains that such cognition is *apramāṇa* because it is sublated by *pramāṇa*, exemplified by the cognition of two moons, and he subsumes it under the kleśa-structure of *avidyā* [[YBh 1.8](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.8)]]. At the root, *avidyā* itself is *viparyasta-khyāti*, taking the *anitya*, *aśuci*, *duḥkha*, and *anātman* as *nitya*, *śuci*, *sukha*, and *ātman*; its removal is through *aviplava-vivekakhyāti* [[YS 2.5](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.5)]][[YS 2.26](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.26)]].

For Nyāya, *mithyājñāna* is the first member in the causal series binding beings to saṃsāra, and *apavarga* follows when *mithyājñāna*, *doṣa*, *pravṛtti*, *janma*, and *duḥkha* are successively removed [[NS 1.1.2](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.2)]]. Vātsyāyana makes the content explicit: *mithyājñāna* ranges across the entire *prameya*-list, taking the non-self as self, duḥkha as sukha, the anitya as nitya, and so on, while *tattvajñāna* is just its *viparyaya* [[NBh 1.1.2](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.2-prose-on-mithyajnana)]]. In the later Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika synthesis, Annambhaṭṭa defines *viparyaya* simply as *mithyājñāna*, exemplified by shell-silver, and the Vaiśeṣika sūtras reduce *avidyā* to defective cognition arising from *indriyadoṣa* and *saṃskāra* [[AnTS 64](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/64)]][[KVS 9.25-27](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/9.25-27)]].

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā treats *mithyā* as an epistemic defect, not as a mode of being. Śabara defines *mithyāpratyaya* as a cognition later sublated by "naitad evam", and invokes that model precisely to deny that Vedic injunctions are revisable in this way [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.2](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-prose-on-mithyapratyaya)]]. When he turns to perceptual error, *mithyājñāna* is traced to impairment in *manas*, *indriya*, or *viṣaya* and corrected by *bādhakajñāna*; the category remains one of *pramā* and *aprāmāṇya*, not ontology [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.5](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-prose-on-mithyajnana)]].

Mādhyamaka does not posit *mithyā* as a third ontological kind; conditioned things are called *mṛṣā* because they are *moṣadharma*, deceptive and dependently arisen [[MMK 13.1](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/13.1)]]. The positive framework is the two truths: *dharmadeśanā* proceeds by *lokasaṃvṛtisatya* and *paramārthasatya*, and *paramārtha* is not taught apart from *vyavahāra* [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8)]][[MMK 24.10](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.10)]]. Accordingly, falsehood lies in *mithyāgrāha*, the naive grasp of *svabhāva*, which Nāgārjuna compares to taking mirage-water as real water [[VV 13](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/13)]].

Yogācāra treats false appearance through *vijñapti* and *vitathavikalpa* rather than through an ontological category of *mithyātva*. In the *Viṃśatikā*, the stock objection notes that dream-objects and the *gandharvanagara* appear as if external although no corresponding external object performs the relevant function, and Vasubandhu replies that *deśa-kāla-niyama* can be secured *svapnavat* without external objects [[VVi on 2](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/on Vvs 2)]][[VVi 3](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Preta Replies)]]. The deeper point comes at VVi 17: the unawakened subject does not grasp the absence of the dream-object, and the worldling, asleep through *vitathavikalpābhyāsavāsanā*, likewise sees *abhūtārtha* until corrected by *lokottara-nirvikalpajñāna* [[VVi 17](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/17-dream-awakening)]].

Pratyabhijñā treats *mithyā* as a defective apprehension within the play of *ābhāsa*, not as an unreal world outside consciousness. Utpaladeva's shell-silver example says that the *rajata-buddhi* is *bhrāntā* or *mithyā* because the *vimarśa* fails *deśa/upādhi-saṃvāda*, while the appearance itself still occurs within the one *pramātṛ* [[ĪPK 2.3.13](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/2.3.13-with-ipv)]]. More fundamentally, *māyīya mala* is the *viparyāsa* by which an originally non-different *vedyam* appears as *bhinna*, producing *janma* and *bhoga* under the Lord's *māyāśakti* [[ĪPK 3.2.5](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/3.2.5-with-ipv)]]. Abhinavagupta accordingly compares *mithyājñāna* to *timira*: the defect stains the seeing, while the luminous field itself remains *vimalam* [[TA 1.331](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/1.331)]].

translator_note: Do not translate *mithyā* as 'non-existent'. Usually avoid the unqualified glosses 'unreal' and 'illusory' as well, because they import either nihilism or a merely psychological notion of illusion. In Advaita the term marks a specific metaphysical status: experientially and transactionally operative, yet not ultimately real in the way *brahman* is. 'Sublatable' is often safer than 'illusory', or else leave the Sanskrit in place and explain it.
